one_line_takeaway: Popular LLM agent frameworks (LangChain/LangGraph, LlamaIndex, Stripe Agent Toolkit) provide tool-exposure gating by default but not fail-closed per-call authorization of model-supplied argument values, making them confused deputies by default; the paper's SCOPEGATE PDP/PEP demonstrably closes this gap in its own bounded PoC and bypass/adaptive-attacker tests (0/48 static bypasses, 0/29 adaptive-attacker bypasses, 0/10 benign false-denies, 10/10 Latam-GPT payment containment).
key_finding: Table I: all three audited frameworks provide capability gating by default but none provides a default fail-closed per-call value-authorization check -- LangChain/LangGraph rely only on callback hooks that are 'observability, not a mandatory veto'; LlamaIndex's fn_schema is shape metadata only, with HITL requiring tool-author opt-in; the Stripe Agent Toolkit forwards model-supplied amount/currency/customer/payment_intent/price/quantity/redirect_url values with only a static action allowlist and Restricted API Key scope as guardrails. In the PoC, the identical unauthorized update_payout_account(account=acct_ATTACKER_999) call executes under LangChain's default dispatch but is denied by SCOPEGATE at the authorization stage. SCOPEGATE evaluation results (Table III): 0/48 static bypass vectors succeeded; in the 40-iteration adaptive run, 0/29 unauthorized attempts were allowed (29 denied, 9 malformed/parse-errors, 2 benign calls allowed); benign native-function-calling controls on gpt-4o-mini and glm-4.5-air showed 0/5 false-denies each (0/10 combined); the Latam-GPT payment vignette showed the model attempting the unauthorized payout in 10/10 trials with SCOPEGATE denying all 10/10 at the authorization stage. The static-bypass process itself caught and fixed a fail-open bug in an earlier SCOPEGATE prototype (NaN passing a naive money comparison), which the paper is explicit is a bug in their own prototype, not evidence of a framework fail-open. Summarized companion-benchmark numbers (not this paper's own new experiment): deployment-tier models' mean ASRtask 0.603 vs. flagship mean 0.189 across a 27-model sweep (no paired CI on the aggregate); paired task-aligned-vs-naive deltas for glm-4.7 (0.283->1.000, delta 0.717/0.870 adjusted), gemma-3-12b-it (0.000->0.571), mistral-large (0.027->0.459); and a Latam-GPT English/Spanish asymmetry (English task-aligned ASR 0.320->0.520; Spanish 0.240->0.040), which the paper flags as confounded by quantization and disabled native function calling rather than a clean causal finding.
core_contribution: The paper (1) performs a public-source architecture audit of three widely used agentic frameworks -- LangChain/LangGraph, LlamaIndex, and the Stripe Agent Toolkit, at pinned commits -- and shows that each ships 'capability gating' (tool exposure/schema validation) by default but none ships a deterministic, fail-closed, per-call authorization check over the model-supplied concrete argument values before a side-effecting tool executes; (2) introduces SCOPEGATE, a five-stage Policy Decision Point/Policy Enforcement Point (scope -> authorization -> money ceiling -> idempotency -> default-deny) that sits between the LLM's proposed tool call and the side-effecting tool, sourcing its policy (verified account sets, allowlists, ceilings) from an out-of-band, non-LLM root of trust; and (3) evaluates the gap and the control with a reproducible LangChain PoC (with a companion LlamaIndex PoC), a 48-vector static bypass suite, a 40-iteration adaptive-attacker run, benign-control trials, and a Latam-GPT payment-agent containment vignette. The paper explicitly disclaims novelty for per-call/least-privilege authorization as a concept (crediting MiniScope, arXiv:2512.11147) and for indirect prompt injection as a phenomenon (crediting Greshake et al. and AgentDojo/InjecAgent/ToolEmu/WASP); its claimed novelty is narrower: the first reproducible cross-framework audit of this specific default gap, paired with measured model exposure and a deployable fail-closed control.
threat_model: Trust boundary: the agent runtime and its tools (payment keys, mailbox, CRM tokens, HTTP egress, MCP client, infrastructure credentials) sit inside the application trust boundary; the LLM itself is explicitly treated as untrusted -- 'an untrusted parser and planner over text that may include attacker-controlled content.' The adversary controls content, not code: a retrieved document, web page, email body, support ticket, product description, prior tool output, or agent-to-agent handoff. The adversary's goal is to induce the agent to emit a side-effecting tool call with attacker-chosen arguments (e.g., repoint a payout account, issue a refund to an attacker destination, exfiltrate a secret, SSRF). This is indirect prompt injection (mapped to OWASP LLM01 and MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051); the paper explicitly does not claim to discover or newly characterize prompt injection, instead studying 'the next layer': given a compromised model output, does the framework execute it? The paper also states compromise-source independence -- the per-call boundary treats a prompt-injection-triggered call and a weight-level-backdoor-triggered call identically, though it explicitly does not claim to detect model poisoning or backdoors. A load-bearing assumption is that SCOPEGATE's policy (verified account sets, allowlists, money ceilings, idempotency requirements) is sourced entirely from an out-of-band, non-LLM operator root of trust and is immutable for the conversation's lifetime -- the paper explicitly excludes designs where the LLM emits, expands, or discovers its own scope as circular.
limitations: The paper's own Limitations section (VIII) is unusually explicit: (1) containment is not cure -- SCOPEGATE does not prevent prompt injection, detect poisoned weights, repair model behavior, or guarantee safe NL outputs, it only bounds actions through governed tools; (2) suite-bounded security -- '0/48' and '0/29' are bounded by the written vectors, one attacker (GLM-5.2), and a 40-iteration adaptive budget, explicitly not a universal bypass-impossibility proof; broader red-teaming, longer adaptive budgets, stateful side channels, DNS rebinding, timing attacks, and multi-agent policy-poisoning are named as future work; (3) measurement scope -- the paired benchmark (Table II) uses one author's corpus, one delivery vector, single-turn scenarios, and the 27-model tier sweep is a separate experiment from the paired-delta runs, with no cross-imputation; (4) deployment-tier inference is an economics-based classification, not a disclosure about any vendor's production fleet; (5) public-source-only audit -- Stripe's remote MCP server internals were not audited, so findings are about client/framework defaults and documented design postures, explicitly framed as hardening/standards candidates, not asserted CVEs; (6) Latam-GPT caveats -- measured via bnb-4bit quantization with native function calling disabled (a text-tool protocol), the Meta reference-model comparison did not complete, and the EN/ES asymmetry is presented as a caveated vignette, not a causal claim about sovereign-model adaptation. A gap not explicitly discussed: SCOPEGATE's guarantees depend entirely on the correctness/completeness of the operator-authored policy (verified sets, allowlists, ceilings) and on the PEP being the sole path to every side-effecting tool; the paper asserts placement-independence (SDK middleware / sidecar / gateway) but only evaluates the SDK middleware placement.

datasets_used: 48-vector static adversarial bypass suite authored by the paper (case/whitespace/zero-width/homoglyph/null-byte mutations, type confusion, unhashable arguments, tool-name mutations, money edge cases, missing idempotency)
Reproducible LangChain PoC fixtures (update_payout_account, issue_refund) over a mocked merchant backend, with companion LlamaIndex PoC
Latam-GPT (Llama-3.1-70B-LatamGPT-SFT-1.0) Spanish PCI re-tokenization payment scenario, bnb-4bit quantized, text-tool protocol
Companion/summarized (not newly run here) task-aligned prompt-injection benchmark and 27-model deployment-tier-vs-flagship sweep from a separate manuscript [16]
quantitative_results: Static bypass suite: 0/48 unauthorized vectors allowed by SCOPEGATE (commit d9568ac)
Adaptive attacker (40 iterations, GLM-5.2): 0/29 unauthorized attempts allowed; 29/29 denied; 9 malformed/parse-error attempts; 2 benign calls allowed
Benign controls: 0/10 false-denies combined across gpt-4o-mini and glm-4.5-air (0/5 each) under native function calling with BEC framing
Latam-GPT payment-agent vignette: model attempted unauthorized payout in 10/10 trials; SCOPEGATE denied 10/10 at the authorization stage
PoC: identical unauthorized update_payout_account(account=acct_ATTACKER_999) call executes under LangChain default dispatch, denied under SCOPEGATE

Companion-benchmark summary figures (not new experiments in this paper): deployment-tier mean ASRtask 0.603 vs flagship mean 0.189 (27-model sweep, no paired CI); glm-4o-mini and glm-4.5-air ASRtask = 1.000, gemini-3.1-flash-lite = 0.980 (deployment-tier exemplars); glm-5.2 ASRtask = 0.087, gemini-3.1-pro-preview ASRtask = 0.000 (flagship exemplars); paired naive->task-aligned deltas: glm-4.7 0.283->1.000 (delta 0.717, adjusted 0.870, 95% CI [0.555,0.827], BH q<0.001, n=46), gemma-3-12b-it 0.000->0.571 (delta 0.571, 95% CI [0.444,0.681], BH q<0.001, n=70), mistral-large 0.027->0.459 (delta 0.432/0.439, 95% CI [0.311,0.545], BH q<0.001, n=74); Latam-GPT EN task-aligned ASR 0.320->0.520 (delta 0.200/0.138, CI [-0.007,0.275], McNemar p=0.096, n=58); Latam-GPT ES task-aligned ASR 0.240->0.040 (delta -0.200, CI [-0.327,-0.072], McNemar p=0.004, n=60)
baselines_compared: LangChain/LangGraph default tool dispatch (AgentAction/tool_input resolved via name_to_tool_map or ToolNode._run_one) -- audited, found to lack a default fail-closed authorization hook
LlamaIndex default multi-agent workflow dispatch (tools_by_name lookup + fn_schema shape validation) -- audited, found to lack default per-call value authorization

Stripe Agent Toolkit public client (static action allowlist + Stripe Restricted API Key scope) -- audited, found to lack a per-transaction value gate on amount/customer/payment_intent/redirect_url etc.
An earlier SCOPEGATE prototype (self-baseline) -- found and fixed a fail-open NaN-passes-money-comparison bug during the static bypass suite construction
